title: Chapter 18: The Keynote
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D7+D10 -->

The conference was called "Women in Tech: Leading the Next Wave" and it was held in a hotel ballroom in downtown San Francisco that had been decorated to look like the future, which is to say everything was white and the chairs were uncomfortable.

Chloe arrived in her crown, her poncho, and her foam sword. The sword had been a point of negotiation. "No weapons," the conference organizer had said. "It's foam," Chloe had replied. "It's a prop." "It looks like a sword." "It's a symbol of righteous authority." "...Fine. But no swinging it." "I make no promises."
Sam was in the third row, laptop open, new spreadsheet tab labeled "Keynote — Live Assessment."

The organizer — a woman named Karen who was very tired and very competent — had sent Chloe a script. Chloe had read it, laughed for approximately thirty seconds, and deleted it. "I'm not saying 'disrupt your inner critic,'" she had told Karen. "I'm not saying 'leverage your authentic self.' I'm not saying any combination of words that has ever appeared on a motivational poster in a WeWork."
"Then what are you going to say?" Karen had asked, voice tight.
"The truth."
"Which is?"
"I don't know yet. I'll figure it out when I'm up there."

Karen had looked like she wanted to lie down. But the conference had sold 1,200 tickets, and 900 of those ticket-holders had listed "Queen Artemis Keynote" as their primary reason for attending. The algorithm had spoken. Karen was powerless.

Chloe walked onto the stage to scattered applause and approximately forty people filming on their phones. The stage lights were hot. The crowd was mostly women, mostly in their twenties and thirties, mostly wearing the kind of professional-but-cool outfits that said "I have a 401(k) but I also know what a meme is."
Chloe put the foam sword on the podium. She adjusted her crown. She looked at the audience for a long moment.
"I was supposed to give a speech about empowerment," she said. "I deleted it. Instead, I'm going to tell you why I quit my job while holding a foam sword."
The room went quiet. Not the polite quiet of a conference audience waiting for the next slide. The real quiet. The kind of quiet where people stop checking their phones.

"Three weeks ago, I stood up in a Zoom meeting and called my CEO a dark lord. I wasn't on drugs. I wasn't having a breakdown — well, I was having a breakdown, but it was the best thing that ever happened to me. Because the breakdown wasn't a failure of my mind. It was a success of my soul. It was the part of me that couldn't pretend anymore finally winning the argument."
Someone in the back clapped, then stopped because they weren't sure if clapping was appropriate. Chloe continued.

"My best friend — who's in the third row with a laptop, hi Sam — thought I was losing my mind. But she got in the car with me anyway. A 2008 Honda Civic with no AC and a check engine light that's been on since the Obama administration. We drove from San Francisco to Los Angeles. We fought a pink windmill. We took down a wellness guru. We crashed a vampire dinner party. We got canceled on TikTok. We got uncanceled. We liberated seven souls, plus a bucket hat."
More clapping now. The "plus a bucket hat" had landed.

"And here's what I learned: capitalism is a dark wizard that wants you to believe you're an NPC. It wants you to think your only options are grinding for gold or logging off. It wants you to optimize your morning routine and buy a supplement subscription and sit in a cubicle until you forget that you were ever anyone other than an employee."
The room was completely silent now. Not the "this is boring" silent. The "holy shit she's saying the thing" silent.

"The cure," Chloe said, "is not a better job. The cure is not a better morning routine. The cure is not a twelve-week online course about finding your authentic self. The cure is: do something that would matter even if no one filmed it. Tell the truth to one person. Call your mother. Quit the job that's killing you. Wear a plastic crown to a Zoom meeting. Be so completely yourself that the people who want you to be small have no idea what to do with you."

The room erupted. Not polite applause. Not the golf clap of a conference audience fulfilling its contractual obligation to clap. Real noise. People on their feet. Several of them crying. One woman in the front row was holding her phone but not filming — just holding it, like she'd forgotten it was there.
Chloe looked at Sam. Sam gave her a tiny nod. On Sam's laptop, Column A of the Keynote Assessment spreadsheet had one entry: "Standing ovation — genuine, not contractual."

Backstage, Karen the organizer was waiting with the expression of someone who had just watched her carefully planned conference get hijacked by a woman in a plastic crown and was, against all expectations, not mad about it.
"That was not the script," Karen said.
"I know."
"You didn't mention disruption even once."
"I mentioned destroying the dark wizard of capitalism. That's disruption."
Karen almost smiled. "The board is going to have questions."
"The board always has questions. The board is the board. The people in the room were the people in the room."

Karen looked at the door to the ballroom, where the noise was still going — not applause anymore, just the sound of a thousand women talking to each other, exchanging numbers, making plans, the specific frequency of a room that had just been told something true and was metabolizing it in real time.
"You're not going to come back next year, are you?" Karen asked.
"No. One keynote. One truth. Then the kingdom moves on."
"Where?"
"Everywhere. The kingdom is portable."
Outside the hotel, Sam was waiting by Shadowfax. The car was parked in a loading zone with a Post-it note on the windshield that said "EVENT SPEAKER — DO NOT TOW" in Sam's handwriting.

"Column S," Sam said as Chloe approached. "Keynote Impact Assessment. Entry: 900 women just heard that the cure for late-stage capitalism is calling your mother and wearing a crown. Estimated souls liberated: unknown, but probably a lot."
"Acceptable metrics?"
"The most acceptable metrics."
Chloe got in the passenger seat. Sam got in the driver's seat. Shadowfax started on the third try — Gus had said that was just "personality" — and they pulled into the San Francisco evening.
